title: Offices of Lawyers

evidence_facts.title: Clio Releases 2025 Legal Trends for Solo and Small Law Firms Report
evidence_facts.quote: Only 8% of solo practitioners and 4% of small law firms have adopted AI widely or universally.

scope.population: Solo practitioners and small law firms in Clio's 2025 report
scope.unit: percent of firms or practitioners reported as widely or universally adopting AI
scope.industry_mismatch: The report covers solo and small law firms rather than every US NAICS 541110 firm or the constructed target band.

evidence_facts.title: Clio's 2025 Legal Trends for Mid-Sized Law Firm Report
evidence_facts.quote: AI adoption has surged from 19% to 93% in just one year, transforming how legal professionals work.

scope.population: Mid-sized law firms in Clio's 2025 report
scope.industry_mismatch: The source's mid-sized-firm definition and adoption definition are not reported in this release and do not map directly to all US NAICS 541110 firms.

evidence_facts.title: 2026 Report on the State of the US Legal Market
evidence_facts.quote: a full 90% of all legal dollars still flow through standard hourly rate arrangements

scope.population: All legal dollars measured in Thomson Reuters Legal Tracker data
scope.period: 2026 report using cited 2025 Legal Department Operations Index data
scope.industry_mismatch: Legal Tracker spending data includes outside-counsel spending and does not isolate US NAICS 541110 target-band firms.

evidence_facts.title: Rule 5.4: Professional Independence of a Lawyer
evidence_facts.quote: A lawyer or law firm shall not share legal fees with a nonlawyer, except that:

scope.population: Lawyers and law firms subject to the ABA Model Rule
scope.industry_mismatch: A model rule is not a census of state adoption or a firm-level measure of US NAICS 541110 economics.

evidence_facts.title: Alternative Business Structure
evidence_facts.quote: An alternative business structure, or ABS, is a business entity that includes nonlawyers who have an economic interest or decision-making authority in a firm and provides legal services in accord with Supreme Court Rules 31 and 31.1(c).

scope.population: Arizona alternative business structures providing legal services
scope.period: current Arizona Judicial Branch program page
scope.industry_mismatch: Arizona ABS regulation is a state-specific exception and does not describe ownership rules or firm counts for US NAICS 541110 generally.

evidence_facts.title: Demographics
evidence_facts.quote: More than 13% of all lawyers — roughly 1 in 8 — are 65 or older.

scope.population: All lawyers in the United States
scope.unit: share of lawyers age 65 or older
scope.period: 2023 Current Population Survey data reported in the ABA profile
scope.industry_mismatch: All lawyers include employees outside law offices and do not report the age of law-firm owners or partners.

scope.population: Reported BizBuySell legal-services and law-firm sales
scope.unit: 13 reported sales; median sale price, revenue and cash flow; average revenue and cash-flow multiples; median days on market
scope.industry_mismatch: The table is a small-business marketplace sample with median cash flow far below the constructed $1-10 million EBITDA target band and reports cash-flow rather than normalized EV/EBITDA.

evidence_facts.title: 2025 Legal Trends Report
evidence_facts.quote: In total, 59% of firms billed flat fees exclusively or in addition to offering an hourly rate in 2024.

scope.population: Law firms represented in Clio's 2025 Legal Trends Report
scope.unit: share of firms using flat fees exclusively or alongside hourly rates
scope.period: 2024 billing data reported in the 2025 report
scope.industry_mismatch: Clio platform and survey populations are not a census of US NAICS 541110 firms.

evidence_facts.title: 2025 Legal Trends Report
evidence_facts.quote: nearly half (45%) have made adjustments to their pricing because of it.

scope.population: Law firms using AI more widely in Clio's 2025 Legal Trends Report
scope.unit: share of wide AI adopters reporting pricing adjustments
scope.industry_mismatch: The source is limited to wide AI adopters and does not quantify retained savings or represent all US NAICS 541110 firms.

role_judgments.rationale: Research, first drafts, document review and routine analysis are exposed, while advocacy, negotiation, client counseling and lawyer professional judgment remain human work.
Document assembly, discovery processing, citation checking and filing preparation are workflow-heavy, though factual investigation and client coordination remain human.
Legal-secretary scheduling, formatting, intake and filing are highly automatable, with human exception and relationship work retained.
General clerical records and document workflows are highly automatable aside from exception processing.
Routine inquiry triage and scheduling can be automated, while sensitive client intake and escalation need people.
Billing, time-entry reconciliation and bookkeeping workflow are automatable, but collections judgment and controls remain human.
Reporting and schedule management are AI-assisted, while supervising legal-support staff and handling exceptions remain human.
Administrative documentation, scheduling and communication are highly automatable apart from executive support and exceptions.
Title-search and abstracting tasks are document and data intensive; this supplied occupation is retained unchanged despite likely sibling-code contamination.
Operations reporting, billing analytics and workflow coordination automate partly, while partner-management and strategic decisions remain human.
The unlisted share is a mixed group of law clerks, IT, marketing, records and litigation support; use a middle estimate without relabeling the supplied occupations.

scope_mismatches: F1 is a North American solo-and-small-firm adoption survey, not an occupation-level task study for all US NAICS 541110 firms.
F3 measures legal dollars in hourly arrangements rather than task exposure by SOC.
F4 is a model professional-conduct rule, not a task-level automation measurement.
The supplied role mix is at four-digit 5411 and includes a title-examiner occupation that may be a sibling-code artifact.
bridge_steps: Use F1 as evidence that smaller law practices are experimenting with document automation and virtual receptionist services rather than universally deploying AI.
Use F3 to distinguish the automatable production of legal work from the billed-hour revenue model.
Use F4 to preserve the boundary around lawyer professional judgment and legal-fee arrangements.
Assign task-level fractions to each supplied SOC without changing any supplied wage share or SOC label.
Use a separate mixed-work fraction for the required residual wage share.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Select higher fractions for document, clerical, billing and title-search workflow than for lawyers and management, whose professional judgment, advocacy and relationship work remain materially human.

pi_dist.rationale: Predominant hourly billing makes efficiency prone to client capture through fewer hours, while flat fees and AI-linked pricing changes allow some firms to retain savings through value or outcome pricing.

scope_mismatches: F3 is legal-spend data rather than a target-band law-office margin study.
F8 and F9 are Clio report populations rather than a census of US NAICS 541110 firms.
None of the sources measures the actual share of AI savings retained by an operator.
bridge_steps: Use F3's hourly-dollar share as evidence that efficiency can reduce the hours sold.
Use F8 as evidence that many firms also use flat fees.
Use F9 as evidence that wider AI adopters are changing pricing, without assuming the direction or magnitude of every change.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Select 0.30 because hourly billing and client cost scrutiny dominate, while alternative-fee migration leaves a partial path to capture operational savings.

pi_moat.rationale: Model Rule 5.4 restricts nonlawyer fee sharing and partnership, but Arizona's ABS program demonstrates that nonlawyer economic interests are possible in a state-specific exception.

scope_mismatches: F4 is a model rule and does not establish adoption or enforcement in each jurisdiction.
F5 is Arizona-specific and does not quantify substitution or entry in the rest of the United States.
Neither source measures client switching, unbundled legal work or firm-level defensibility.
bridge_steps: Treat F4 as evidence of a structural constraint on ordinary nonlawyer ownership and fee sharing.
Treat F5 as evidence that the ownership restriction has a state-specific regulatory exception.
Do not equate either regulatory fact with a measure of demand durability.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Select 0.45 for a partial ownership and professional-independence barrier that constrains conventional roll-ups but does not prevent regulatory exceptions or product substitution.

t50_years.rationale: Wide or universal AI adoption is low in solo and small firms but reported very high in Clio's mid-sized-firm release; the divergent populations require a forecast rather than a direct extrapolation.

scope_mismatches: F1 covers North American solo and small firms, while F2's mid-sized-firm definition and geography are not reported in its release.
Neither source measures the pipeline's US NAICS 541110 material-use threshold or supplies a time series for that exact population.
bridge_steps: Use F1 as evidence of low wide adoption in the many smaller practices that dominate the broad law-office population.
Use F2 as evidence that adoption can move faster in larger, better-resourced practices.
Do not calculate a blended firm rate because the sources do not supply comparable population weights or definitions.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Forecast five years from the run date to reach 50% material firm adoption, reflecting rapid capability improvement but uneven small-practice implementation.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: The 4%-8% wide-adoption figures for solo and small firms are the closest broad-population anchor, while the 93% mid-sized figure is not transferable without a stated size definition or weighting.

scope_mismatches: F1 is a North American solo-and-small-firm report rather than all US NAICS 541110 firms.
F2's mid-sized-firm population is not defined in the cited release and may not match either the overall NAICS population or the target band.
Neither quote defines material use in automatable functions.
bridge_steps: Use F1's 4%-8% wide-or-universal adoption as a low adoption anchor.
Use F2 to recognize that larger firms may be materially ahead without treating it as an industry-weighted average.
Set a modest allowance above the small-firm anchor for partial material deployment outside the widely-or-universally category.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Select 0.10 as a cautious estimate for US NAICS 541110 material AI adoption, not as an observed firm-share statistic.

historical_analogs.value: No quantified historical legal-technology analogue was found in the permitted live research. The observed gap between solo and small firms and the cited mid-sized-firm release is used only as current size-segment context, not as a causal historical adoption curve.
historical_analogs.rationale: The cited observations are current and non-comparable by population, so they cannot validate a historical diffusion rate.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: No numerical historical analogue; adoption timing remains judgmental.

scope_mismatches: Neither cited source is a historical adoption study for the US NAICS 541110 population.
The two sources use different firm-size populations and do not supply comparable methodologies in the quoted material.
bridge_steps: Use F1 and F2 only to identify present uneven adoption by firm scale.
Do not transform the current cross-sections into a historical growth-rate calculation.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: State the absence of a usable historical analogue rather than assert an unsupported comparison.

selection.rationale: The ABA reports 13.1% of all lawyers are 65 or older and 17.9% are 55-64, but it does not identify firm owners or isolate ages 60-64; owner 60-plus therefore requires a judgmental mapping.

scope_mismatches: F6 covers all lawyers, including non-owners and lawyers outside law offices.
The source gives age bands 55-64 and 65-plus, not an exact 60-plus owner share.
The source period is 2023 while the run date is 2026.
bridge_steps: Use the 65-plus share as a hard lower-age anchor for an older-lawyer population.
Recognize the 55-64 band contains some but not all lawyers aged 60-plus.
Do not infer that every older lawyer is an owner or that every owner is a lawyer in the source population.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Select 0.25 as an owner-60-plus estimate above the 65-plus lower anchor but below the combined 55-plus lawyer share.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: No directly verified source located in the permitted live searches documents a succession shortage for the US NAICS 541110 target population.
succession_shortage_documented.plausible_range: false absent directly documented target-population shortage evidence

scope_mismatches: none; no source fact is used
bridge_steps: Searches performed: ABA legal-profession age profile, ABA law-firm succession-planning materials, and public law-firm transition coverage.
The located materials established age and succession-planning context but not a directly documented shortage for the US NAICS 541110 target population.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Treat the absence of located direct target-population shortage evidence as an estimate, not proof that no shortage exists.

active_consolidators.rationale: No disclosed, directly countable US target-band consolidator population was located; nonlawyer ownership restrictions make a zero count an absence-of-evidence estimate rather than an observation.
active_consolidators.plausible_range: 0-10 disclosed active platforms under a strict US law-office control definition

scope_mismatches: none; no source fact is used
bridge_steps: Searches performed: ABA Model Rule 5.4 ownership restrictions, Arizona ABS program and directory materials, public legal private-equity and MSO coverage, and small-law-firm acquisition searches.
The searches found ownership-rule context and Arizona ABS activity, but not a countable comparable population of active US target-band consolidators.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Select zero as a conservative absence-of-evidence estimate for the strict count definition, while recognizing MSO and ABS structures may exist outside it.

buy_mult.rationale: BizBuySell reports a 1.87 cash-flow multiple for a small sample whose median cash flow is far below the target band; 2.5x EV/EBITDA is a judgmental normalized target-band entry figure, not an observed comparable.

scope_mismatches: F7 reports 13 small-business marketplace sales, not the US $1-10 million EBITDA target band.
F7 reports cash-flow multiples, not enterprise-value EBITDA multiples after market-rate owner compensation.
Target geography and legal-practice mix are not reported in the cited row.
bridge_steps: Use F7 only as evidence that public small-practice transaction data are sparse and low-multiple on a cash-flow basis.
Recognize that owner compensation embedded in seller cash flow must be normalized to market rate before an EBITDA comparison.
Do not mechanically convert 1.87x cash flow to EV/EBITDA because that conversion requires unsupported owner-pay and capital-structure assumptions.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Select 2.5x as a conservative normalized-entry estimate above the cited small-cash-flow multiple but explicitly not as an observed target-band transaction multiple.

exit_mult.rationale: No public target-band exit comp was located; 3.5x assumes only a modest premium over entry for a lawyer-owned platform after owner-compensation normalization and retention risk.

scope_mismatches: F7 reports 13 small-business sales outside the target band and cash-flow, not normalized EV/EBITDA, multiples.
The cited row does not identify a platform buyer, exit population, client-retention outcome or owner-compensation normalization.
bridge_steps: Use F7 solely as the sparse public small-law-firm transaction reference.
Require market-rate owner compensation conceptually before comparing EBITDA, while acknowledging no calculation can be made from the row.
Allow a limited platform premium only as a judgment because ownership constraints and portable-client risk prevent assuming a conventional roll-up exit.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Select 3.5x as a cautious estimated exit multiple, leaving a modest spread over the estimated entry multiple without claiming a verified platform multiple.

pricing_structure.value: Predominantly hourly billing, increasingly supplemented by flat or alternative fees; AI efficiency threatens billable hours unless firms convert work to value-based pricing or increase volume.
pricing_structure.rationale: Thomson Reuters reports most legal dollars still flow through hourly arrangements, while Clio documents flat-fee use and pricing changes among wider AI adopters; applying these sources to the full code requires judgment.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Hourly-led model with material practice-area variation in flat, contingency and alternative fees

scope_mismatches: F3 is legal-spend data and F8-F9 are Clio report populations, neither a full NAICS 541110 revenue census.
None of the sources reports the exact fee mix of the constructed target band or separates contingency practices.
bridge_steps: Use F3 as the evidence of hourly-billing predominance.
Use F8 as evidence that flat fees are already common alongside hourly fees.
Use F9 as evidence that wide AI adopters are changing pricing in response to AI.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Describe the code as hourly-led but mixed rather than attach an unsupported single fixed-fee or contingency percentage.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: The role estimate identifies large document and support-work exposure, but low wide adoption in small practices and uneven deployment by firm scale support a positive multi-year t50. Python supplies uplift_pp.

terminal_value.justification: AI can lower the cost of producing legal work while lawyer professional-independence rules constrain ordinary nonlawyer ownership. Yet the predominantly hourly revenue base means fewer hours can shrink the paid product, and alternative-fee conversion or demand expansion may only partly offset that deflation.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V and A use legal-work adoption, workflow and professional-rule evidence only; ownership-rule and sparse transaction evidence are used only for B and M.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: The hourly-billing evidence is mapped to a low pi_dist because clients can capture efficiency through fewer billed hours; pricing-model change and regulatory ownership constraints support only partial pi_moat.

dataset_mismatch.description: The injected four-digit 5411 role mix includes title examiners, abstractors and searchers, which may belong chiefly to sibling activities rather than pure law offices; partner-owner distributions may also sit outside W-2 wages. The injected labor share and role weights are used unchanged.

sensitivity.alternative_value: Set the supplied title-examiner role fraction to 0.30 instead of 0.75 while retaining its injected 0.0082 wage share
sensitivity.impact: The role aggregate would fall by about 0.0037; the larger uncertainty remains the unmeasured partner-draw and four-digit-mix distortion.

sensitivity.alternative_value: Partner-owner compensation outside W-2 wages is materially higher or lower than the injected labor-share construction
sensitivity.impact: V_raw changes proportionally, but the deterministic labor_share is not adjusted in this run.

subsegments.name: Hourly-billed business, corporate, transactional and litigation boutiques
subsegments.evidence_or_uncertainty: Selected as dominant because the legal-market source identifies hourly arrangements as the dominant legal-dollar model; it does not provide a NAICS revenue split by practice area.

subsegments.name: Plaintiff contingency-fee practices
subsegments.evidence_or_uncertainty: Included because contingency fees have different AI price deflation mechanics; its NAICS revenue rank is a judgment not reported in the cited sources.

subsegments.name: Consumer and small-business flat-fee practices
subsegments.evidence_or_uncertainty: Included because flat-fee and direct-consumer automation exposure differ from hourly work; exact NAICS revenue share is not reported.

market_boundary.dominant_subsegment: Hourly-billed business, corporate, transactional and litigation boutiques
market_boundary.selection_basis: Score the hourly-billed boutique segment because the live legal-dollar pricing evidence is most directly relevant to it, and disclose that contingency and flat-fee practices have materially different price-deflation mechanisms.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A equals 10 divided by t50, so adoption timing materially changes A.
The current level determines whether the t50 boundary is plausible and evidences uneven deployment across firm sizes.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: Entry 1.5x-4.0x and exit 2.0x-5.0x EV/EBITDA after market-rate owner compensation
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: The sparse, non-comparable public transaction reference makes M highly sensitive to normalization and the assumed spread.

reviewer_note: All task fractions, target-population adoption mappings, owner-age mapping, zero consolidator count, pricing capture and normalized EV/EBITDA multiples are explicit estimates.
